message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Over-wide version range for arize-phoenix (>=7.0,<18) admits 10 major versions
DIMENSION: dependency & supply chain
SEVERITY: LOW
FILE: pyproject.toml 55
DESCRIPTION: `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` spans eleven major versions (7 through 17). The installed/resolved version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the floor. A range this wide defeats the point of the cap: any breaking change or a freshly-disclosed advisory in any major 7-17 is admitted by a clean install, and the engine has no lockfile to constrain it (see lockfile finding). Other extras are reasonably bounded (single-major caps), making this one an outlier. Same family is also slightly inconsistent: langgraph>=1.2,<2 is duplicated across the `persistence` and `orchestration` extras (functionally fine, but two sources of truth for one bound).
SUGGESTED FIX: Tighten the phoenix cap to the majors actually tested (e.g. `>=17,<18`, matching the resolved 17.6.0) or whatever range integration CI truly exercises, and de-duplicate the langgraph bound (define it once and reference it) to avoid drift.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

2	requires = ["hatchling"]

6	name = "assay-engine"

8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "thehipsterciso" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",

30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/thehipsterciso/hc-assay"

42	    "ruff>=0.6",
43	    "mypy>=1.11",
44	]
45	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
46	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
47	reasoning = [
48	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
49	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
50	    "anyio>=4,<5",
51	]
52	observability = [
53	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
54	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
55	    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",

57	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",

59	persistence = [

61	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
62	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
63	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",

65	orchestration = [

71	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
72	all = [
73	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",

78

88	src = ["src", "tests"]
89	
90	[tool.mypy]

92	# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
93	packages = ["assay_engine"]

content.text: The finding is factually accurate about line 55. Let me verify the claims about how phoenix is actually used and whether there's a lockfile.

content.text: No lockfile exists, confirming that part. Let me look at the actual phoenix usage in tracing.py to understand the API surface being exercised.

content.content:
40	
41	
42	def tracing_base_url() -> str:
43	    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
44	    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"
45	
46	
47	def tracing_endpoint() -> str:
48	    """OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint at the local collector (loopback-enforced)."""
49	    return f"{tracing_base_url()}/v1/traces"
50	
51	
52	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
53	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
54	
55	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
56	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
57	    """
58	    global _provider
59	    if _disabled():
60	        return None
61	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
62	    with _provider_lock:
63	        if _provider is not None:
64	            return _provider
65	        try:
66	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
67	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
68	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
69	            from phoenix.otel import register
70	
71	            provider = register(
72	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
73	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
74	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
75	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
76	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
77	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
78	            )
79	            _provider = provider
80	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
81	            _instrument_langchain()
82	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
83	            return _provider
84	        except Exception:
85	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced.
86	            return None
87	
88	
89	def _warn_if_not_global(provider: Any) -> None:
90	    """If another provider already won the global slot, our manual spans would vanish (#O6)."""
91	    try:
92	        from opentelemetry import trace
93	
94	        if trace.get_tracer_provider() is not provider:
95	            _log.warning(
96	                "assay tracing: another OpenTelemetry provider holds the global slot; "
97	                "manually-emitted spans may not reach the local collector"
98	            )
99	    except Exception:

content.text:
Confirmed: installed version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the `>=7.0` floor. The whole try/except wraps every failure mode (missing extra, collector down, API change) and degrades gracefully by returning None.

Let me check the git history/docs for any justification of the wide range, and check how the langgraph duplication is treated.

content.content:
1389242 harden/pass-1: 22 production-readiness fixes (assess→2-agent verify→fix→2-agent confirm) (#123)
f9d8cb4 feat: complete the composition layer + public-readiness (audit close-out) (#98)
26d5c57 test(hardening): exercise the platform against real backends + fix what mocks hid (#72)
0f69735 feat(orchestration): goal-agnostic graph + gate interrupt/resume wiring (#59)
f4c2798 feat(persistence): checkpointer + vector store + local data versioning (#49)
---PHOENIX HISTORY---
commit ff827ae7b46ae89fcfa5686043b3f7de4ec8d670
Date:   Mon Jun 15 21:33:01 2026 -0400
+    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",
commit 3c41b3bcfa408a24399655070cf5915103af1e3d
Date:   Mon Jun 15 21:15:36 2026 -0400
+    "arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18",

Exit code 1
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:75:- **Problem:** The integration job's final step runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov` flag is provided by the `pytest-cov` plugin, but pytest-cov appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — not in the `dev` extra (which only pins pytest, ruff, mypy) and not pulled transitively by any heavy extra. The lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]`, none of which provide pytest-cov or coverage. I verified in a clean Python 3.14 venv: (1) `pip install 'pytest>=8'` does not bring pytest_cov (`importlib.util.find_spec('pytest_cov')` is None); (2) running `pytest --cov=assay_engine` with no plugin exits with `ERROR: ... unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine`; (3) dry-run resolving mlflow+langgraph+arize-phoenix surfaces no pytest-cov/coverage. Therefore the entire integration lane — the only lane that exercises the real backends (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, live Postgres + Qdrant containers) — fails at the test step on every PR/push. The repo's CI is effectively only running the dependency-free core lane; the backend code paths are never green-gated.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:78:- **Verifier 2:** TRUE positive, independently reproduced. .github/workflows/ci.yml:73 runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov`/`--cov-report` flags are provided only by the pytest-cov plugin. pyproject.toml's `dev` extra (lines 39-43) declares only pytest>=8, ruff>=0.6, mypy>=1.11; grep over pyproject.toml and .github/ confirms pytest-cov/coverage are declared nowhere, and there is no requirements*.txt/setup.cfg. The integration lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]` — a `pip install --dry-run` of the heavy deps (mlflow, langgraph, arize-phoenix) surfaces NO pytest-cov or coverage transitively. (The dev's local .venv does contain pytest_cov 7.1.0, but `pip show pytest-cov` lists `Required-by:` empty — it was installed standalone, so it is not provided by any project dependency and would be absent in clean CI; this is the trap that can make a naive local check look green.) PoC in a fresh venv containing only pytest: `pytest --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` fails with `error: unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine` and exits code 4 — i.e., the integration job's only test step fails before collecting/running 
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:153:The transitive-surface claim is verified against the live venv: 217 installed packages, including cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, requests 2.34.2, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, mlflow 3.13.0, arize-phoenix 17.6.0 — all pulled transitively via the observability/persistence/reasoning extras in pyproject.toml. The integration lane (ci.yml lines 62-65) does a fresh, unpinned `pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"` on every run, so a future CVE'd transitive can land silently with n
---ADR---
ARCHITECTURE.md
CHARTER.md
GLOSSARY.md
GOVERNANCE.md
METHODOLOGY.md
decisions
hardening
protocol

content.text: The integration lane does an unpinned fresh install on every run, confirmed by pass-1 docs. The phoenix line was added/edited during pass-1 hardening (Jun 15), not pre-existing. Let me check the pass-1 doc context around this phoenix line and confirm the langgraph duplication claim is harmless.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git show ff827ae --stat | head -20; echo "==="; git log --oneline ff827ae -1
input.description: Inspect commit that set phoenix range

content.content:
commit ff827ae7b46ae89fcfa5686043b3f7de4ec8d670
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Mon Jun 15 21:33:01 2026 -0400

    feat(observability): port self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking (#41)
    
    * feat(observability): port self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking
    
    Second infra port (ADR-0003). Generalized from hc-grc's Phoenix/OTel +
    MLflow wiring; all on-box, dataset-agnostic (ASSAY_* env), backends optional
    (observability extra) and lazily imported so the engine loads/tests without them.
    
    Tracing: bootstrap_tracing() idempotently wires the global OTel provider to a
    loopback-bound local collector (the reasoning seam's spans now land there);
    run_trace_context(run_id) stamps spans; OtelTracer emits spans (no-op without
    OTel); UnconfiguredTracer fails loud. Disabled via ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING;
    degrades gracefully (never load-bearing).
    
    Tracking: MlflowExperimentTracker (local SQLite store, lazy mlflow) implements
    the ExperimentTracker Protocol; get_tracking_uri resolves relative sqlite to
===
ff827ae feat(observability): port self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking (#41)

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:75:- **Problem:** The integration job's final step runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov` flag is provided by the `pytest-cov` plugin, but pytest-cov appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — not in the `dev` extra (which only pins pytest, ruff, mypy) and not pulled transitively by any heavy extra. The lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]`, none of which provide pytest-cov or coverage. I verified in a clean Python 3.14 venv: (1) `pip install 'pytest>=8'` does not bring pytest_cov (`importlib.util.find_spec('pytest_cov')` is None); (2) running `pytest --cov=assay_engine` with no plugin exits with `ERROR: ... unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine`; (3) dry-run resolving mlflow+langgraph+arize-phoenix surfaces no pytest-cov/coverage. Therefore the entire integration lane — the only lane that exercises the real backends (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, live Postgres + Qdrant containers) — fails at the test step on every PR/push. The repo's CI is effectively only running the dependency-free core lane; the backend code paths are never green-gated.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:78:- **Verifier 2:** TRUE positive, independently reproduced. .github/workflows/ci.yml:73 runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov`/`--cov-report` flags are provided only by the pytest-cov plugin. pyproject.toml's `dev` extra (lines 39-43) declares only pytest>=8, ruff>=0.6, mypy>=1.11; grep over pyproject.toml and .github/ confirms pytest-cov/coverage are declared nowhere, and there is no requirements*.txt/setup.cfg. The integration lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]` — a `pip install --dry-run` of the heavy deps (mlflow, langgraph, arize-phoenix) surfaces NO pytest-cov or coverage transitively. (The dev's local .venv does contain pytest_cov 7.1.0, but `pip show pytest-cov` lists `Required-by:` empty — it was installed standalone, so it is not provided by any project dependency and would be absent in clean CI; this is the trap that can make a naive local check look green.) PoC in a fresh venv containing only pytest: `pytest --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` fails with `error: unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine` and exits code 4 — i.e., the integration job's only test step fails before collecting/running 
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:153:The transitive-surface claim is verified against the live venv: 217 installed packages, including cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, requests 2.34.2, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, mlflow 3.13.0, arize-phoenix 17.6.0 — all pulled transitively via the observability/persistence/reasoning extras in pyproject.toml. The integration lane (ci.yml lines 62-65) does a fresh, unpinned `pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"` on every run, so a future CVE'd transitive can land silently with n

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Let me assess.

**Facts confirmed:**
1. Line 55: `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` — spans majors 7-17 (11 majors). Accurate.
2. Resolved version is 17.6.0 — ten majors above floor. Accurate.
3. No lockfile exists; CI does unpinned fresh install on every run. Confirmed via pass-1 docs and filesystem check.
4. Other extras are single-major capped (langgraph `<2`, mlflow `<4`, psycopg `<4`, qdrant `<2`, numpy `<3`, langchain-ollama `<2`). The phoenix `<18` over `>=7.0` is the lone outlier — accurate.
5. langgraph `>=1.2,<2` is duplicated across `persistence` and `orchestration` extras. Accurate but functionally harmless (identical bound, same source intent).

**Severity assessment:**
The core question is whether this is a genuine production-readiness defect. Key mitigating realities:
- The only consumer of phoenix is `phoenix.otel.register(...)` inside `bootstrap_tracing()`, which is wrapped in a broad `try/except Exception` that returns `None` on any failure. Tracing is explicitly never load-bearing ("degrades gracefully"). So even an API-breaking major would not break a study run — it would silently drop tracing.
- That said, the actual supply-chain concern (a phoenix advisory in some admitted major auto-installed into CI's integration lane, which IS the lane running real backends) is real but already largely subsumed by the broader pass-1 finding #153, which flagged the unpinned-fresh-install / no-lockfile transitive surface across the whole observability/persistence stack. The phoenix range is a narrow instance of that already-known systemic gap.

This is a real but minor hygiene issue. The wide range is genuinely inconsistent with sibling deps and does undercut the cap's intent. It's a legitimate LOW. It is not already fixed, not documented-justified (no rationale anywhere for 7.0 floor), so `is_real=true`. But it does not threaten production correctness given the graceful-degradation wrapper, and the supply-chain angle overlaps an already-recorded finding. LOW is the correct calibration — arguably the lower end of LOW.

input.reasoning:
Independently confirmed and a genuine (minor) dependency-hygiene defect, correctly rated LOW.

FACTS VERIFIED:
- pyproject.toml:55 declares `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18`, spanning eleven majors (7-17). The floor/cap span is real, not a misread.
- Resolved/installed version in the project venv is 17.6.0 (confirmed via `pip show arize-phoenix` and `phoenix.__version__`), i.e. ten majors above the `>=7.0` floor — the floor is essentially dead.
- No lockfile or requirements*.txt exists (filesystem scan returned nothing), and the CI integration lane does an unpinned fresh `pip install -e ".[...]"` on every run (corroborated by pass-1 docs PASS-1.md:153). So the wide range is genuinely the operative constraint at install time, not backstopped by pinning.
- Outlier claim is accurate: every sibling cap is a single-major window — langgraph `<2`, mlflow `<4`, psycopg `<4`, qdrant-client `<2`, numpy `<3`, langchain-ollama `<2`, claude-agent-sdk `<0.3`. Phoenix `>=7.0,<18` is the lone wide range.
- langgraph `>=1.2,<2` duplication across the `persistence` (line 60) and `orchestration` (line 66) extras is real; functionally harmless (identical bound) but is two sources of truth that can drift.
- No documented justification anywhere (git history, ADRs, docs/) for the 7.0 floor; it is not an intentional/documented scope decision.

WHY ONLY LOW (recalibration upheld):
- The sole consumer of phoenix is `phoenix.otel.register(...)` inside `bootstrap_tracing()` (tracing.py:69), wrapped in a broad `try/except Exception` that returns `None` on ANY failure. Tracing is explicitly never load-bearing and degrades gracefully, so even an API-breaking phoenix major cannot break a study run — it would silently drop tracing, not crash the engine.
- The real supply-chain angle (an advisory in some admitted major auto-pulled into the integration lane) is a narrow instance of the already-recorded systemic gap: unpinned fresh install + no lockfile across the whole observability/persistence transitive surface (pass-1 #153). The phoenix range is one symptom of that broader, already-known issue rather than an independent high-impact defect.

Net: real, not already fixed, not documented-justified — so is_real=true. But impact is contained by graceful degradation and overlaps an existing finding, so LOW (lower end) is correct. The suggested fix (tighten to `>=17,<18` matching what's tested, and define the langgraph bound once) is reasonable.
input.evidence: pyproject.toml:55 `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` (11 majors). Installed: `pip show arize-phoenix` -> Version 17.6.0; `phoenix.__version__` == 17.6.0 (ten majors above floor). Sibling caps all single-major: pyproject.toml:57 mlflow<4, :60/:66 langgraph<2, :62 psycopg<4, :63 qdrant-client<2, :69 numpy<3, :48 langchain-ollama<2. No lockfile/requirements*.txt on disk; PASS-1.md:153 confirms unpinned fresh `pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"` per CI run. Graceful-degradation containment: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:65-86 — `from phoenix.otel import register` inside try/except Exception returning None ("Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced"). langgraph dup at pyproject.toml:60 and :66. git log -S confirms phoenix line introduced Jun 15 2026 (commit ff827ae) with no version-range rationale.

content.text:
Verified. The finding is a genuine but minor dependency-hygiene defect, correctly rated LOW.

Summary of my independent verification:
- `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` (pyproject.toml:55) does span 11 majors; the resolved version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the floor — the floor is effectively dead.
- It is a true outlier: every sibling extra uses a single-major cap.
- No lockfile exists and CI does an unpinned fresh install per run, so the wide range is the operative install-time constraint.
- The langgraph `>=1.2,<2` duplication (lines 60 and 66) is real but functionally harmless.

Why it stays LOW rather than higher: phoenix's only consumer is `phoenix.otel.register(...)` inside `bootstrap_tracing()` (tracing.py:65-86), wrapped in a broad try/except that returns None on any failure — tracing is never load-bearing, so even an API-breaking major can't break a run. The supply-chain angle is a narrow instance of the already-recorded systemic no-lockfile/unpinned-install gap (pass-1 #153). Real, not already fixed, not documented-justified, but low impact.
